# Transcrição: 03-V-Scale

- **Fonte:** 03-V-Scale
- **Duração:** 14:05
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

There are many situations where fast scaling is not the right thing to do. And in those situations, vertical scaling or V scale is often the easiest and most predictable way to just get your budgets up. It's lower risk and it has more predictability built into it because you're not impacting Facebook's machine as much as bigger budget increases go or duplicating campaigns and things like that. So it's a fairly low risk way of increasing your budgets. One thing that Facebook have advised is that right now if you increase your budgets less than 40%, then the machine is less impacted by the change in the budget. But also the time of day that you scale up makes a difference as well. And other factors around optimization and funnel, everything would be through the BPM method. All of those things in place by this point, it makes V scaling so much more successful. Not everyone needs to 10x their spend, 100x their spend right away. And especially if you've never increased your budgets before and you've never really tested scaling, then V scaling and just doing vertical scaling on existing campaigns and ad sets is far less risky than introducing some of the different ways of scaling, which we'll cover off as well. So when it comes to vertical scaling, we're making moderate budget increases and creating stability and predictability with our campaigns. So as we've discovered before, in the previous videos, there are different ways of vertical scaling your existing campaigns. You could increase your budgets once a day and you might bump it 20%, 30% or you could do it multiple times in a day, especially when you're using campaign budget optimization because of the way it handles those budgets as well. But if you've never started scaling before, then this is the place to start. To keep things moderate, then I would maybe, if it's the first time you've done it, do a 20% increase in your budget. So using campaign budget optimization, you'd edit your campaign, you know, if you're spending $100 a day, change it to 120. Doesn't seem like a lot, but you can just monitor and see what's going on. For some of you, it just, you know, isn't appealing to add $20 extra on your budget. And that's fine. There's different strategies for different mindsets as well. When it comes to increasing the budgets, one thing that is important to understand is Facebook's pacing algorithm. What this essentially does is based on your ad account time zone. So if you're based in Europe, US, wherever it is, you have to set a time zone in your ad account and that dictates how Facebook's pacing algorithm works. So that basically looks at a 24 hour clock and predicts when it should spend your budget.

## [03:04 - 06:05]

Because if it didn't and you had $100 and you start spending that midnight, then chances are by one minute past midnight, you could easily burn through $100 and your budget's gone. So pacing is built in to help spread your budget in a day, especially when you're using daily budgets. Now, lifetime budgets is a whole different ballgame where you set a time span and you set your budget and say, spend this within this time span. So when you're using lifetime budgets, you might say, right, I want to spend $10,000 in the next two weeks. So you put that into your lifetime budget. What Facebook will do is figure out the best days to spend the money and manage the budgets on a daily basis for you. Whereas the standard way of setting budgets is literally, I want to spend $100 a day, regardless of performance. And that's why you have to optimize and keep an eye on your ad account. It's not to say that lifetime budgets are a complete solution and we'll cover that off as well. But there are different ways of setting your budgets. Now, if you're using daily budgets, then, you know, doing 20 to 30% bumps a day will have a direct impact. If you're using lifetime budgets, then it won't because you're still working within that two week parameter. So for example, one of the downsides of lifetime budgets being that if you have a great performance today, you can't react to it as fast as you can with daily budgets. So that's just something to bear in mind. But then when you apply the budget changes on a standard budget setup on a daily basis, makes a big difference. So generally, I would increase my budgets as early in the day as possible, considering the ad account time zone. So if I am running an ad account in the UK, for example, I would want to make my budget increases before 9am because that gives me as much time, much more time to monitor and track that performance in the day. So if I start at 9am, then I've got until 12am that day to make to analyze that performance. That's 15 hours to track and monitor. Some people would say do it at midnight. The only problem is if it's the first time you've done it, you've not got the proper tracking in place. You don't know how scaling works. And, you know, you're not able to track your account at midnight. Then that could be a risk because there's a few reasons. Number one, if you do increase your budget at midnight, which some people do advise, risk number one is you're not around, let's say until 6, 7, 8, 9 o'clock in the morning to see what's happened and things might not work out. Secondly, although your ad account is based on a 24 hour clock, your users will rarely access your business based on the 25, 24 hour clock. So it's not like, you know, you're browsing to buy a hat at 11 o'clock at night and you're like, I've only got one hour to buy because at 12 o'clock, the algorithm is going to reset. And I want to make sure I got my purchase in this day and not that day.

## [06:05 - 09:09]

Fact is people that you've been targeting today may decide to buy at midnight. They might decide to buy at 11 30 p.m. They might decide to buy at 1 a.m. They are yesterday's customers that are transacting in today. The problem with the pacing algorithm is if you've increased your budgets at midnight and you've got a hangover of customers on your site, clicking through your ads at, let's say 11 50, 11 55 and throughout the day. Previously, that budget could be spent quite quickly because Facebook still sees that there's good buyer intent. But the problem is that those buyers are from the previous day and your new budget might go and try and find similar buyers, but they're not around because they were targeted earlier in the previous day. And therefore the performance starts to decrease. So that's why I prefer to make my budget changes generally after 6 a.m. ad account time. And especially if I'm doing it for the first time on an account, then I want to be around and that's why I choose 9 a.m. So it's really, you know, partly about how early you are at rising, but then making sure I'm starting my new day based on new customers that are going to be visiting my site, which for most most ad accounts will be kind of four or five, six a.m. and seven a.m. onwards as well. So it's just something to bear in mind when you are doing your budget increases, doing it at the right time of the day. I might do it up to 11 in the morning, 12 p.m. All the way up to maybe two, three p.m. in the evening in the afternoon time. But I would rarely do it in the late afternoon or evening, partly because of the pacing algorithm, because what you're doing there is you're saying, I want to increase my budget 30% at, let's say, 6 p.m. in the evening. Facebook now has six hours to figure out how it's going to spend that extra 30% because you're not saying to Facebook, spend it if the performance is good. You're saying to Facebook, spend it regardless. So that's the problem with increasing your budgets too late. So just bear that in mind as well. Now, if you've got a big campaign running and, you know, your sale ends at midnight or something like that, that's the different matter. And in that case, I would be increasing my budgets potentially all the way through the evening, but I'm also keeping an eye on them, whether through ads manager directly or through my KPI spreadsheets, just to make sure that I'm there to perform actions that may be required to maintain that performance as well. I'm also tracking that performance and understanding the feedback of the data of what I'm seeing as well. So that if I know that, you know, if I increase budgets at 9 a.m., it has a better impact than doing it at 6 a.m. They're all part of your learning because when we look at creating automation, and you may or may not want to do this, I need to understand the pulse of the ad account. Every ad account has a different pulse. What I mean by that is I could take 10 very similar businesses with similar

## [09:09 - 12:09]

targeting, landers, funnels, performance, et cetera. But when I come to scaling, the performance will be different. And in fact, I've seen this happen across accounts that targeting very similar users for different products and scaling works in different ways based on many different factors, like we've discussed the auction and the algorithm and things like that. So before I even consider putting scaling rules in place, I want to make sure I really understand the performance because the worst thing about automating rules is it will do exactly as you say. And sometimes what you think you want is not what you want. And we'll cover that off as part of the different parts of the training when we talk about different scaling methodologies and also in the automation section, you'll see what could go wrong as well. So it's really important that you write your rules down manually, whether it's in a spreadsheet, piece of paper or a Word document, and you're practicing it and you see that performance before you start to try and automate it. So for example, here's a real basic rule that we apply when it comes to scaling. So if your campaign is performing consistently well for three days, not including today, so the last three days, and you're within your target, whether that's cost per acquisition, return on ad spend, cost per registration, cost per lead, cost per install, whatever your target is. If you've had three days of good performance and yesterday's performance is also good. Now, the reason I do that is you could have good performance over three days, but maybe the last day was far worse than the previous two, which means you're on a downward trend. In that case, I don't want to increase my budgets. So I've got a check in place that says three days is good and yesterday's good. Then increase my budget and in this case, 30% at 9 a.m. in the morning. Now, it could be 6 a.m. could be 7 a.m. etc. But this is how I would run something manually. I would train my media buyers up and get them to practice this and apply it, understand how the data is working, do over three, four, five, 10 days before we even consider automating it so that we build the stability right in from the start. And we're able to create better results by through our automations and have that confidence in what we do. Now, the second part of the scaling is what we call what I look at is called scale testing. This is where I go through the graduation process. I've gone through profitability and stability. I have my three to five days of consistently good results, which means my KPIs, my key performance indicators are all on track. Now what I want to do is see how my campaign reacts to scaling so that my scale testing comes ahead of proper scaling. So in this scenario, I'm working in phase three of the graduation process.

## [12:09 - 14:05]

I'm working with the existing campaigns and I'm V scaling it at least twice within a week. So that means within a seven day period, maybe 10 days, I want to at least increase my budget by 20, 30% on a given day because I want to prove that I can push that budget up. So for example, if I'm spending $1,000 day, I'm going to bump it to 1200, give it a day or two, make sure that I've got consistent results and then bump that again to extra 20%, 30% see what kind of performance comes out. I will also combine that with the Punisher method and Add Sniper, which you've seen in the optimization section, which means I'm pausing my bad performing ads and restyling the next day if required. And if I see a drop in overall campaign performance, then I drop the budget down by 10% and I'm stabilizing it after I've done my V scaling as well. And if my results are maintained, it means that I've passed my scale testing mode and I'm confident now that I can really push these harder. This is not a required step, but it's a step that builds confidence for me, for my team, especially in the agency where we are not running our own spends to make sure that we have a control in place before we go ahead with scaling. Now, with my personal accounts, I don't always do this because I'm happy to take the risk of scaling faster and seeing if I get the same performance. But this extra control is much better to have in place. If, for example, you don't have the budgets to lose on scaling or you're managing someone else's budget, it's just a safer way of introducing scaling ahead of really going all out on whichever scaling strategy you want to do as well. So scale testing is really important for me as a step after graduation testing, after profitability and stability to be ready for the next step of scaling, whatever that looks like for you.
